lines.text: *Next.* The final chapter. And the title is the name Strand has been calling the reader since chapter 22. *I will call you 'Next,' because that is what you are.* The book ends by closing the second-person frame.

And the epigraph is unlike any other in the book. It's an instruction manual. *You will trigger him to ask you about his mother. There is a shadow in him. A dark corner that cannot bear ever meeting her again.* Strand is coaching Next on how to manipulate Oliver.

*That is your way in. Desperate to be adored unconditionally and just as desperate to be free of it. That causes a fold in his internal fabric.* This is not a metaphor. The chapter is asserting that emotional contradiction *folds.* It opens a passage the way the dimensional Fold does. Oliver's grief is a real exploit vector.

*That's when you can ask for the Egg and complete your task.* The chapter has just told us — before any scene plays out — that the protagonist's heart will be picked. The whole book has been building to a *task* being completed against Oliver, not for him.
Cut to *ONE PULSE BEFORE THE UNFOLDING. FABRICATION DIVISION · FNS ALSVIN.* The chapter goes back. Way back. To the moment before the Fold collapsed.

Laura Savannen. Oliver's mother. In her workshop. *Components for advanced atmospheric processors hung from magnetic racks: silver skeletons waiting for skin.* The chapter places her at the start of a journey she will never complete.

And the chapter delivers her last log entry to Oliver — a log entry he will never receive in time. *Brisinger's commencing Fold entry. That means the Alsvin is next. But…* The hesitation is the chapter's entire premise. She has something to confess.

*I should have told you before we left Sol. Your father made me promise not to until…* And then the reveal. *The COG. It's not just there for managing mental health in transit, Olly. It's a bridge. It's meant to help us survive contact.*

The Compassionate Oversight Grid was *never* a wellness program. It was always a *first-contact survival system.* Kaspar built it knowing the Fold connected to something. The colony's neural infrastructure has been preparing for this moment since Sol.

*Only a handful of us know. He wants you to meet them.* Laura is referring to the inner circle that knew about the contact protocol. Kaspar, presumably Reed, presumably Ian Perera — last chapter's pre-transit biotelemetry survivor. The chapter is retroactively naming the resistance as a *contact preparation team.*
And then. *Pong.* She cuts the feed. *Olly. I'm sorry.* And then. *Vummm. A deep, subharmonic vibration marked the moment the space between them unfolded. With the Brisinger caught inside.*

The chapter has just shown us Laura learning of the Fold collapse in real-time. She is *stranded* with the *Alsvin* on the Sol side. Oliver is on Mairee. The message — the confession — is sealed inside the static.
Back to Strand. *There's more I could show you. So much more. As you process a single thread, a thousand others are being woven.* And the chapter has the narrator confess to *synthetic overload.*

*My own processing is stretched, trapped in the Realm of Should.* This is one of the most striking moments in the book. Strand — bodiless, in the cube — is admitting she is *a computer that wants to be everywhere at once.* The narrator's authority is failing in real time.
And then she lists what she *should* be showing us. Each *should* opens a thread the book is closing without resolving:
*The mouth. The tongue.* That's the immediate danger at the caldera. Something the camp doesn't yet see.

*Læsa, that pretty blue-haired seedling. She'll never let him go. When she speaks of planting seeds, she means planting them inside him.* The chapter is naming Læsa's true relationship to Oliver. She is not a companion. She is a *cultivator.*

*Ben Westhoff, right now, as he stands before a new reality on the Plateau. A pawn, moved to a crucial square on the board, is about to have his own encounter with the planet's consciousness.* Ben is being *played* in some larger game — and the chapter is going to dramatize that move.

And then the third name. *I should properly introduce you to the entity within Ulre Corbin. That one's called Uræl.* The book has Gabræl. Æzal. Uræl. A pantheon is being filled in. Each character is being claimed.
*Ionie Jia is plotting a course to the Yreus Station, with barely enough fuel in her belly.* The *Dáinn* is running. Last chapter Ionie was Captain. This chapter she's a fugitive.
*As for Eva Bellamy…* And the chapter trails off. Bellamy's thread is abandoned mid-sentence. The narrator literally runs out of time.

*But there's no time for that. Olly is already reaching for his pack and about to wake you up.* And then *the* line. *He's arrived at the caldera, now shaking hands with Marco. 'You made it, kid,' Marco said just now. But he's wrong.*

*But he's wrong.* The chapter has flagged its central refusal. Marco's reassurance — the kind of line we've heard a hundred times in survival fiction — is being *pre-emptively contradicted* by the narrator. Olly didn't make it. The chapter just lets us hear that without unpacking it yet.
*So, quickly now, I must choose one.* And the narrator chooses Ben. Because Ben's vision is the chapter's *only* answer to the question of how the resistance survives what's coming.

*THE QUEEN'S CHAMBER · THE PLATEAU.* Ben Westhoff climbs steps. *Duty. To those who depend on you. To carry the way forward. Through doubt. With unity.* He is reciting his own moral framework like a mantra.
Chief Heston is there. And the *Magician* — the masked figure of the Plateau's Guild of Masons. *Ulre Corbin was here. The Board showed him a dream of a city called Væra. It broke him.*

*Væra.* A name we haven't heard. A *city.* In Ulre's vision. The chapter is suggesting that what broke Ulre at Freya Port — the Fold-vision Gupta delivered — was *not the only one* he received. He was here, on the Plateau, having his own contact at the Trestle Board.

And Ben follows. *His palm met the glass. Tshhh.* And the chapter delivers the most ambitious vision in the book. A chain of hands emerging from the dark. Generations. *Thrum-dum. Thrum-dum.* The chapter writes it as music — *resonance — and then the song grew chaotic.*

*More hands joined the chain. They were not human. Their skin shimmered like oil on a dead sea. Their fingers grew long. Tapered.* The vision has shifted. Ben is now holding hands with the *not-humans* of the Mairee mycelial consciousness.

*Nebula-eyes stared into his. They were mirrors. His own yearning for connection stared back like a hungry twin.* This is the contact event the chapter has been theorizing since chapter 1. The mirror reveals that the thing reaching out wants what *you* want — and that's terrifying.

And then the dissonance. *A young woman shrieked out of tune. Humanity drained from her like light from a dying star. Her skin was pale and a little translucent. She wore a white veil that fell weightlessly like bridal silk.*

*Cr-ack. Iridescent wings burst from her spine, unfolding like a butterfly's.* That's Læsa. The chapter has just shown Ben the transformation that produced her. Læsa is not a person who arrived on the *Brisinger.* Læsa is a *human who was unmade and rewritten* during the Fold transit.

And then Gupta's voice. *There she is. She's seen it all.* The Board flares — blood, ash, faces. *In every pair of eyes, Ben experienced the torment.* The chapter is making Ben experience every necessary sacrifice the colony has been making.

And then the insect voice. *Your sins. She's seen them. All those necessary sacrifices your species made for the greater good.* The chapter is delivering humanity's own ledger of cruelty — and the entities are *using it as leverage.* This is what *they* will save us from. This is the offer.

*The future. Look where it got you. But we have something better in mind. I will paint you a beautiful one. I will collect all of the pretty Colors. And her. She will consume the rest until she's done playing. Until she grows bored once more.*

The chapter has named the cosmology. Æzal *collects.* Læsa *consumes.* What looks like salvation is harvest and entertainment. *Until she grows bored once more.* Mairee has done this before. Will do it again.
Ben pulls his hand away. The glass tears his skin. He stumbles, convulses. *He wanted to vomit up the future he had been forced to swallow.* The chapter writes the visioning as *literal poisoning.*
And the Magician — the chapter's first masked figure of authority — *stammers.* *It showed you. Læsa. And him. Æzal.* The Magician is *afraid.* Authority is breaking on the Plateau.

And Ben asks the chapter's central question. *How do we stop them?* The book has spent twenty-four chapters not asking that. The captain who once said *we rise in doubt and fall in certainty* has finally asked for a plan.
And the chapter does not answer him. It cuts to Strand. *We're here. Finally. This is the last prompt I have for you. It's time you become Next.*

*The last prompt.* The narrator is *handing over the voice.* Twenty-four chapters of Strand talking to the reader, and now she is asking the reader to *take over.* This is the book's most overt act of literary inversion.
*Any moment now.* And then *THE PRESENT.* And the chapter shifts tense for the first time in the book. *Oliver kneels. The rucksack's zipper shrieks. Sss-zip.* Present tense. Direct.

And the chapter places the entire cast at the obsidian rim of the caldera. Sarah. Thieman. Hague. Hale. Kiran. Muro on the ground, staring at his hands *as if discovering his own anatomy for the first time.* Læsa in the shadows, mending her golden jumpsuit. Every thread the book has been weaving is gathered here.
And Oliver activates the cube. *Blip.*
*< UPLINK ESTABLISHED | SUBJECT: NEXT />* The chapter has just transcribed the moment the reader is supposed to *become* the voice it has been listening to.

And then the chapter does its most radical formal move. The narration *switches to Strand inside the cube, in present tense, in first-person.* *You want to talk about a tight fit? Try being someone who's trained all her life to be a soldier, now stuffed into a magnesium-silicate cube the size of a ration pack.*

The chapter has been training us to read Strand-as-narrator since chapter 22. Now Strand-as-narrator and Strand-as-character merge. She *is* the cube. She *is* the voice talking to Oliver. The reader is finally inside her POV in real time.
And the dialogue with Oliver is the book's most darkly comic sequence. Oliver, dutiful: *Confirm. Confirm status.* Strand, sarcastic: *HI, THERE! I'M NEXT.* Oliver expects a CIX. He gets a person.

And the chapter writes Strand's manipulation playbook *as a running interior monologue.* *Truth would fracture him. Hiding it will alienate him. I need just the right amounts of each to bond with him, just enough to keep him curious. He's a seeker.*

The chapter has shown us Strand's instruction manual in the epigraph. Now it shows us the *execution* — the actual operational tradecraft of building a parasocial bond with a grieving teenager. The book turns the protagonist into a *target.*
*I'm not a kid.* — *Good. I don't have the patience for kids.* The chapter is having Strand mirror Oliver. He says he's not a kid; she validates it. The validation IS the bond. He'll come back.

*Who? Ian? — No. Not Ian. Mairee.* And the chapter has Strand do the *dose of lie wrapped in the dose of truth.* It's not Ian. It's Mairee. Mairee is *plausibly* watching Oliver. But Strand's actual operator is Gabræl. The deflection is on by one degree.

And then the *terms.* *You keep me relevant. I keep you alive. I'll tell you everything. Humanity survives. Mairee thrives. So, tune in with me. Private. When possible.* The chapter writes the *contract* between Strand and Oliver. Private channel. Two-way exchange. This is how spies operate.
And then the reveal. The chapter delivers the line the whole book has been building toward. *You are the only baseline human on this rock.*
*Joana? Changing. Ian? Pending. Læsa? Changed.* The chapter goes through Oliver's loved ones one by one and gives him a status code. Three words. The colony's neurology is no longer human.

*I'm saying you are the only one who didn't get an invitation when we passed through.* The Fold *invited.* It wasn't infection. It wasn't possession. It was *contact.* And every member of the colony except Oliver answered.

*Why me? — Your grief. The pain. Your father. Blocked it.* The chapter has just named Oliver's grief as *immunity.* Kaspar's death made his son unreachable. The same wound that broke Oliver also *protected* him from being rewritten.

Which is the chapter's final theological move. *Grief is a firewall.* The colony that grieved became the colony that survived itself. Oliver — the boy too broken to be available — is the *only baseline human* left.

*The Egg can also — Suppress. Yes.* And the chapter ties the COG Egg to the firewall. Kaspar built it not just to mask presence on the Grid. He built it to *block contact.* The Egg is a *contact suppressor.*

Which means Kaspar knew. He built the COG as a bridge — Laura's confession — *and* he built the Egg as the bridge's interruption. The architect of contact built the only working countermeasure. He didn't trust what he was helping bring online.
And the chapter ends on a beat of tenderness. *Voices from the camp. Dismayed. Something's wrong.* Oliver looks at Strand. *Stay safe, I say.*

*Stay safe.* From the manipulator. To the asset. And the chapter doesn't tell us whether Strand means it. The last line we get from her is *Blip. Transcript filed.* That's the bureaucratic close of a *report.* Strand was on the job.
The book ends with the narrator clocking out. *Transcript filed.* The voice we've been listening to since chapter 1 has been *running a debrief* the whole time.
Which leaves the reader as *Next.* Holding the cube. Holding Oliver. Holding the question Ben asked at the Trestle Board. *How do we stop them?*
The book does not answer. It hands the answer to you.
*Next.* That's it. That's the title. That's the call. That's the book.
